## [03:01 - 06:01]

We used to have slots in our computer that we slid these into the slot. And it was sort of like we use CD-ROMs now. Although you might not even know what CD-ROMs are. But it had data on it. In fact, my first computer didn't even have a hard drive. It just used bigger floppies with a floppy disk. So anyways, this is a floppy disk. And I bought this floppy disk. I actually got an email advertisement. You probably can't read it. It says, turn your computer into a money machine. And it's copyrighted in 1995. So I think I first heard about this. And it talked about how you could create special reports and send them out and sell them online. That was before the internet. It was, well, it wasn't before the internet. ## [06:01 - 09:04]

And it was all about information marketing and how people have made fortunes. How you can make money by selling information. And how people over the years had made fortunes by selling information. And so, of course, I thought it was a scam, right? But I kept on thinking about it. It just made so much sense. Because back in those days, I was studying the stock market. Non-stop. I wanted to be a financial trader. That's all I wanted to do was be a financial trader. So I had gone, you couldn't go look up old stock prices online. So I had gone and looked up old stock prices at the library. That's another thing that some people might not really know what it is anymore. And they had, at this library I would go to in Lakewood, Colorado, they had the Wall Street Journal. They had actually this stuff called MicroFish that went back 100 years. So you could get these little tiny slips and you'd slide them in this machine and then you could read them. And I knew, so I knew the Wall Street Journal went back over 100 years. It started around 1896. So I mean, I was like, okay, if they've been around for 100 years, that must be a decent business. Someone's making money if they've been publishing that newspaper for 100 years. So it was obvious to me that there was money to be made by publishing. By selling information. If others had done it, then I knew I could. So I went ahead and I bought this thing. After I thought about it, I printed it out. I read that thing day after day for, I don't know, almost a week or two. I hid it from my wife. I was scared, but I still thought it was a scam, but I bought this floppy drive. And this had a bunch of information about how to turn your computer into a money machine. I took the information I learned on this. And this cost me, this floppy drive, cost me, I think it was $95 plus $4.50 shipping and handling. So it cost me $99.50. And at that time, I had no money. My disposable income for the entire year at that point, I had a budget. My disposable income for the entire year was $400. And here I was going to spend $99.50 on this one floppy drive. It seemed insane, but I went ahead and I did it. And that has made all the difference. This is good quality information. This thing doesn't even work anymore. If I tried plugging, I found an old computer, I put it in, and it's not there anymore. It's like, you know, this memory degrades, like magnetic media can degrade over time. It doesn't work anymore, but it was good, solid information about information marketing. And I took that and I used it to start my information marketing business. And since then, I've sold, I don't know, well over $20 million of information, all based on this $99.50 investment, which seemed like a crazy investment at the time.
